title: Fix skipped notes in cursor-based pagination
subtitle: PR #128 · notes-api

gate.prompt: Before this fix, why could a note silently disappear from every page of results?

options.text: The cursor was built from the created_at timestamp alone, and ties on that column let rows fall on the wrong side of the cursor
The database occasionally deleted rows during a page load
The API cached the first page and never refreshed it
The limit value was set too low

gate.explanation: A cursor built from a non-unique column can't tell same-timestamp rows apart, so a strict greater-than comparison drops whichever of the tied rows lands on the wrong side.

gate.prompt: What makes the pair (created_at, id) a safe cursor where created_at alone was not?

options.text: It's twice as long, so collisions are half as likely
id is unique, so the pair gives every row a distinct, total order even when timestamps tie
It lets the database skip sorting the rows
It stores the cursor as an integer instead of a string

gate.explanation: Tuple comparison falls back to id only when created_at ties, so every row still has a unique position in the order — nothing to skip or duplicate.

gate.prompt: Why paginate with a cursor at all instead of OFFSET/LIMIT?

options.text: Cursors are shorter to type in a URL
OFFSET re-counts rows from the start each time, so inserts or deletes ahead of the page shift results and get slower on later pages
OFFSET isn't supported by most SQL databases
Cursors don't require an ORDER BY clause

gate.explanation: A cursor anchors to a row, not a row count, so it stays correct and fast no matter how many rows are inserted or removed ahead of it.

sections.heading: Background
sections.summary: notes-api paginates GET /notes with a cursor rather than page numbers. A support ticket reported notes that existed but never appeared in any page of results. The cause: two notes created in the same bulk import shared a created_at timestamp, and the pagination query silently dropped one of them.
sections.body:
GET /notes returns notes newest-first, one page at a time. Each page's response includes a next_cursor the client passes back to get the next page:

GET /notes?limit=20
→ 20 notes, next_cursor = 'MjAyNi0wNy0xMFQwOTowMDowMFo='

GET /notes?limit=20&cursor=MjAyNi0wNy0xMFQwOTowMDowMFo=
→ next 20 notes, next_cursor = '...'

A user imported 40 notes from a backup in one batch. All 40 got the exact same created_at timestamp, because the import set it once for the whole batch. Several of those notes never showed up in the app, no matter which page the user loaded.

sections.heading: Intuition
sections.summary: The old cursor was just a timestamp: give me everything after this moment. When several notes share that exact moment, after-this-moment can't distinguish between them, so a strict greater-than comparison puts some on the wrong side of the boundary forever.
sections.body:
09:00:00
note A
09:00:00
note B
09:00:00
note C
← page 1 cursor lands here

Page 1 ends partway through the 09:00:00 group, say right after note B. Its next_cursor is just 09:00:00. Page 2 asks for everything with created_at > 09:00:00 — but note C also has created_at = 09:00:00, so it fails that check too. Note C is gone: it was never returned on page 1 (page 1 ended before it), and it's excluded from page 2 (it doesn't satisfy strictly-greater-than).

The fix gives every row a second, unique tiebreaker — its id — so the cursor becomes a pair: (created_at, id). Two notes can share a timestamp, but never an id, so the pair always has a definite order.

sections.heading: The code
sections.summary: The query's WHERE clause changed from a single-column comparison to a tuple comparison, and the cursor now encodes both fields instead of just the timestamp.
sections.body:
notes/pagination.py

Before — comparing on created_at alone:

SELECT * FROM notes
WHERE created_at > :cursor_created_at
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT :limit

After — a row-value (tuple) comparison, which most SQL databases support directly:

SELECT * FROM notes
WHERE (created_at, id) < (:cursor_created_at, :cursor_id)
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT :limit

The cursor returned to the client is now a base64 blob encoding both fields:

def encode_cursor(note):
    return b64encode(f'{note.created_at.isoformat()}|{note.id}'.encode()).decode()

def decode_cursor(cursor):
    ts, note_id = b64decode(cursor).decode().split('|')
    return parse_datetime(ts), int(note_id)

Note the ORDER BY also gained id DESC as a secondary sort key — the comparison and the ordering have to agree, or the same skipping bug comes back in a different shape.

sections.heading: Testing the fix
sections.summary: The regression test inserts several notes with an identical, explicitly-set created_at and asserts that paging through with limit=1 returns every one of them exactly once.
sections.body:
tests/test_pagination.py

The bug only shows up with tied timestamps, so the test forces a tie rather than hoping one occurs naturally:

def test_pagination_handles_tied_timestamps():
    same_ts = datetime(2026, 7, 10, 9, 0, 0)
    notes = [create_note(created_at=same_ts) for _ in range(5)]

    seen = []
    cursor = None
    for _ in range(10):  # more iterations than pages, to catch an infinite loop
        page = get_notes(limit=1, cursor=cursor)
        seen += page.items
        if not page.next_cursor:
            break
        cursor = page.next_cursor

    assert {n.id for n in seen} == {n.id for n in notes}
    assert len(seen) == len(notes)  # no duplicates either

The two asserts matter for different reasons: the set comparison catches skipped rows, and the length check catches the opposite failure — a row coming back twice because it landed on both sides of a boundary.

quiz.prompt: A bulk import creates 40 notes that all share the same created_at. What did the old pagination do?

options.text: Returned all 40 correctly, just in a different order
Threw a 500 error on the second page
Silently dropped some of the 40 from every page
Returned all 40 twice

quiz.explanation: Rows exactly at the page boundary's timestamp could fail the strict greater-than check on the next page after already being excluded from the current one — dropped, not duplicated.

quiz.prompt: Why does the fixed query sort by created_at DESC, id DESC instead of just created_at DESC?

options.text: It makes the query run faster
So the ORDER BY agrees with the tuple comparison in the WHERE clause — otherwise rows could still be skipped
It's required syntax for row-value comparisons
So id is included in the response payload

quiz.explanation: The sort order and the boundary comparison must use the same key, or a row could sort on one side of the cursor but compare on the other.

quiz.prompt: What does the new cursor encode?

options.text: Just the note's id
Just the created_at timestamp
Both created_at and id
The full note content

quiz.explanation: Encoding both fields is what gives every row a unique position, even when timestamps tie.

quiz.prompt: Why does the regression test insert notes with an explicitly identical timestamp rather than just inserting them normally?

options.text: To make the test run faster
Because the bug only reproduces when timestamps tie, which normal inserts might not do reliably
Because the API requires a created_at parameter
To test sorting order instead of pagination

quiz.explanation: Forcing the tie makes the regression test deterministic instead of depending on chance clock collisions.

quiz.prompt: Why prefer a cursor over OFFSET/LIMIT for this endpoint?

options.text: OFFSET is deprecated in SQL
A cursor anchors to a specific row, so results stay correct and fast even as rows are inserted or deleted ahead of the page
OFFSET can't be combined with ORDER BY
Cursors use less memory on the client

quiz.explanation: OFFSET counts from the start of the result set every time, so it drifts under concurrent writes and gets slower for later pages; a cursor doesn't have either problem.
